name: Ama-no-Murakumo
description: Ama-no-Murakumo, also known as Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (草薙の剣, lit. 'Grass-Cutting Sword'), is a legendary Japanese sword and one of the Three Imperial Regalia of Japan. Its history is deeply intertwined with Japanese mythology, particularly the tales of the storm god Susanoo and the Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. The sword is believed to possess incredible power, including the ability to control the wind and grant its wielder invincibility. Its absence from public display and the lack of definitive proof of its existence contribute to its mystique and enduring importance in Japanese culture. While its physical form remains shrouded in mystery, its symbolic weight as a representation of imperial authority and divine power is undeniable. Legends say the sword was found within the Yamata no Orochi after Susanoo slew the beast. The sword is considered a national treasure, adding to its cultural signficance.
subtitle: The Grass-Cutting Sword, one of the Three Imperial Regalia of Japan.
origin: Found within the body of the Yamata no Orochi after it was slain by Susanoo.
currentLocation: Atsuta Shrine, Nagoya, Japan (though its presence is unconfirmed)

keyMyths.title: Susanoo and the Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susanoo, the storm god, was banished from the heavens. He encountered an elderly couple who were weeping because they were about to sacrifice their eighth daughter to the Yamata no Orochi. Susanoo offered to slay the monster in exchange for their daughter's hand in marriage. He intoxicated the serpent with sake, and then, while it was incapacitated, he cut it to pieces. From within the serpent's tail, he discovered the Ama-no-Murakumo.

keyMyths.title: Yamato Takeru and the Burning Plains
keyMyths.description: Prince Yamato Takeru was sent to conquer the eastern lands. He was betrayed by a local chieftain who lured him into a grassy plain and set it ablaze. Yamato Takeru used the Ama-no-Murakumo to cut away the grass and create a path to escape the fire. It was during this incident that the sword acquired the name Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, meaning 'Grass-Cutting Sword'. He then used the sword to defeat his enemies and return victorious.

keyMyths.title: The Loss and Recovery of the Sword
keyMyths.description: According to some accounts, the Ama-no-Murakumo was briefly lost at sea. Empress Jingu's army was sailing to invade Korea, and a storm threatened to sink their ships. The sword was thrown into the sea as an offering to the gods, and the storm subsided. The sword was later recovered, solidifying its reputation as a powerful artifact with divine protection, and helping ensure a successful invasion.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, the oldest extant chronicle of Japan, providing valuable insights into the myths surrounding the Ama-no-Murakumo's origin.
sources.text: An English translation of the Kojiki, the oldest extant chronicle of Japan, providing valuable insights into the myths surrounding the Ama-no-Murakumo's origin.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important chronicle that offers a different perspective on the sword's history and its association with Yamato Takeru.
sources.text: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important chronicle that offers a different perspective on the sword's history and its association with Yamato Takeru.

sources.author: Helen Craig McCullough
sources.description: An English translation of the Heike Monogatari, a war epic that narrates the Genpei War and includes references to the sword and its symbolic importance.
sources.text: An English translation of the Heike Monogatari, a war epic that narrates the Genpei War and includes references to the sword and its symbolic importance.

variants: sword of the gathering clouds of heaven

worshipPractices.name: Imperial Rituals
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, Ama-no-Murakumo is revered as one of the Three Imperial Regalia, essential symbols of the Emperor's authority and legitimacy. Its presence is required at important enthronement ceremonies and other significant imperial events, signifying the continuity of the imperial line and connection to the divine.

worshipPractices.name: Shrine Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Though not accessible to the public, it is believed that offerings and prayers are made to the sword within Atsuta Shrine, where it is said to be housed. These offerings are likely performed by Shinto priests to ensure the sword's continued protection and benevolence.

festivals.name: Atsuta Festival
festivals.description: Atsuta Festival, held at Atsuta Shrine, indirectly honors Ama-no-Murakumo as the shrine is believed to house the sword. The festival features traditional dances, music, and processions, celebrating the shrine's history and the deities enshrined within, subtly acknowledging the sword's presence.

cultural.modernLegacy: Ama-no-Murakumo's legend continues to inspire countless works of fiction, from anime and manga to video games and literature, often appearing as a powerful and iconic weapon. The sword represents themes of courage, divine power, and the enduring legacy of Japanese mythology, captivating audiences worldwide.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are signature items associated with powerful deities (Susanoo and Odin, respectively) and represent authority or divine right.
Both are items of immense power that offer protection and are linked to a major deity or hero; Kusanagi protects its wielder, while the Aegis is associated with Zeus and Athena and offers defense.
Both are legendary swords imbued with magical properties and associated with heroic figures; both weapons are deeply rooted in their respective cultures' mythology and represent power and destiny.

associations.name: Wind
associations.significance: Represents the swiftness and cutting power of the sword, capable of felling enemies and clearing obstacles like a strong gust.

associations.name: Grass
associations.significance: Directly related to the sword's alternative name, Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, and its legendary ability to cut through grass effortlessly.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Linked to the Yamata no Orochi, the eight-headed serpent from which the sword was retrieved, symbolizing power and overcoming monstrous challenges.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: Represents purity, divinity, and the sacred nature of the sword as one of the Imperial Regalia.

associations.name: Storm
associations.significance: Connects to Susanoo, the storm god who originally found the sword, highlighting its divine origins and association with powerful forces of nature.

associations.name: Kingship
associations.significance: As one of the Three Imperial Regalia, it symbolizes the authority and legitimacy of the Japanese emperor, embodying power and leadership.

associations.name: Eight
associations.significance: Relates to the eight heads of Yamata no Orochi, the serpent slain by Susanoo, representing the vanquishing of chaos and evil.

companions.name: Susanoo
companions.description: The storm god Susanoo originally found Ama-no-Murakumo inside the Yamata no Orochi. He wielded it to slay the dragon, discovering its sharpness and power.

companions.name: Yamato Takeru
companions.description: The legendary prince Yamato Takeru used Ama-no-Murakumo to cut through a field of burning grass, a feat which earned the sword its later name, Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi. He carried and relied on the sword throughout his journeys.

companions.name: Miyazu Hime
companions.description: Miyazu Hime, Yamato Takeru's aunt, received the sword when Yamato Takeru left to subdue the East. She kept the sword safe and enshrined it, inadvertently protecting it from a sea curse that afflicted Yamato Takeru.

companions.name: Yamata no Orochi
companions.description: While not a companion in the traditional sense, Ama-no-Murakumo was found within the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi, signifying its origin from a powerful, chaotic being. The dragon's defeat led to the sword's emergence.

companions.name: Atsuta Shrine
companions.description: Atsuta Shrine is the traditional home of the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi (Ama-no-Murakumo), where it is enshrined and protected. The shrine itself can be considered a protector of the blade.

extendedContent.title: Creation & Forging: From Serpent's Belly to Imperial Treasure
extendedContent.content:
The tale of Ama-no-Murakumo's origin begins with the storm god Susanoo, brother of Amaterasu, the sun goddess. Banished from the heavens, Susanoo descended to Earth in Izumo Province, where he encountered an elderly couple lamenting the imminent demise of their daughters to the monstrous Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent. Susanoo, promising to slay the beast, devised a plan: he filled eight large sake casks and presented them to the Orochi. As the serpent drank, it became intoxicated and vulnerable. Seizing the opportunity, Susanoo attacked and slew the creature.

While slaying the Yamata no Orochi, Susanoo discovered Ama-no-Murakumo within one of the serpent's tails. The sword possessed an extraordinary sharpness and a peculiar ability to cut through anything with ease. Recognizing its divine nature, Susanoo presented the sword to his sister, Amaterasu, as a peace offering, hoping to reconcile with her and be readmitted to the celestial realm. This act marked the sword's transition from a tool of vengeance to a symbol of reconciliation and imperial power.

The sword's forging is not explicitly described in the Kojiki or Nihon Shoki, the primary sources for Japanese mythology. The narrative focuses on its discovery and presentation rather than its creation. However, the fact that it was found within the body of a monstrous serpent suggests a potential connection to the underworld or to chaotic forces, adding another layer of mystique to its already impressive legend. It is the transition to a tool of peace and alliance that solidifies its place as a symbol of imperial authority.

extendedContent.title: Powers & Properties: Cutting Through Grass and Controlling the Wind
extendedContent.content:
Ama-no-Murakumo, later known as Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, derives its name, 'Grass-Cutting Sword,' from an incident where Prince Yamato Takeru used the sword to cut through a field of tall grass that had been set ablaze by his enemies. This event demonstrated the sword's exceptional sharpness and protective qualities, cementing its legendary status. Beyond its literal sharpness, the sword is also believed to possess spiritual power, capable of influencing the elements, particularly wind and weather.

The sword's powers are not limited to physical prowess. Stories suggest that it can ward off evil spirits and protect its wielder from harm. Some accounts even claim that the sword has a will of its own, guiding its user in times of need. However, its power comes with responsibility; it is said that only a pure and righteous individual can wield the sword without facing dire consequences. A corrupt or unworthy wielder risks incurring the sword's wrath or succumbing to its power.

The precise nature and extent of Ama-no-Murakumo's powers are shrouded in mystery and vary across different interpretations of the myths. While some emphasize its physical capabilities, others focus on its spiritual significance and its role as a symbol of imperial authority. Regardless of the specific interpretation, the sword remains a potent symbol of power, protection, and the delicate balance between the physical and spiritual realms. Its ability to cut through adversity, both literal and figurative, makes it a powerful emblem of Japanese identity.

extendedContent.title: Notable Wielders: From Gods to Emperors
extendedContent.content:
Initially wielded by the storm god Susanoo, Ama-no-Murakumo’s ownership transitioned to Amaterasu, the sun goddess, as a peace offering. This transfer marked its integration into the Imperial Regalia, symbolizing divine authority and imperial legitimacy. However, the most famous wielder in legend is Prince Yamato Takeru, a legendary figure of Japanese history and mythology, known for his bravery and military prowess. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, Yamato Takeru received the sword from his aunt, Princess Yamato-hime, the high priestess of Ise Shrine, before embarking on his military campaigns.

It was during one of these campaigns that Yamato Takeru faced the aforementioned ambush where his enemies surrounded him in a burning field of grass. Using Ama-no-Murakumo, he slashed through the vegetation, creating a path to escape and saving his life. This incident led to the sword being renamed Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, solidifying its association with the prince and his legendary exploits. The sword became an extension of Yamato Takeru's strength and cunning, further enhancing his heroic image.

While emperors are considered the symbolic wielders of the sword as part of the Imperial Regalia, there are no historical accounts of emperors actively using it in battle or other events. The sword's primary significance lies in its symbolic representation of imperial power and divine mandate, passed down through generations. Its presence during enthronement ceremonies underscores the emperor's legitimacy and connection to the divine lineage.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism: Imperial Authority, Divine Mandate, and National Identity
extendedContent.content:
Ama-no-Murakumo, as one of the Three Imperial Regalia of Japan, is a profound symbol of imperial authority and divine mandate. Alongside the mirror Yata no Kagami and the jewel Yasakani no Magatama, the sword represents the power, wisdom, and benevolence required of a just ruler. Its presence during enthronement ceremonies signifies the emperor's legitimacy and his connection to the ancestral gods. The sword's association with Susanoo and Amaterasu further reinforces this divine lineage, linking the imperial family to the very foundations of Japanese mythology.

The sword also embodies the virtues of courage, strength, and protection. Its legendary sharpness and ability to cut through adversity make it a symbol of resilience and the ability to overcome challenges. The story of Yamato Takeru using the sword to escape the burning field of grass illustrates this symbolism, representing the triumph of good over evil and the ability to find a path forward even in the face of overwhelming odds. The Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi serves as a constant reminder of these values, inspiring a sense of national pride and unity.

Beyond its symbolic representation of imperial power and national identity, Ama-no-Murakumo also touches on deeper themes of balance and harmony. The sword's origin in the chaotic realm of the Yamata no Orochi and its subsequent integration into the Imperial Regalia suggests a process of transforming chaos into order. This transformation reflects the ideal of a ruler who can bring stability and prosperity to the nation, harnessing the forces of nature for the benefit of the people. It is this complex interplay of symbolism that makes Ama-no-Murakumo such an enduring and powerful icon in Japanese culture.

shortDescription: The Ama-no-Murakumo, or Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, is a legendary sword from Japanese mythology, famed for its association with the storm god Susanoo.
